text: The surgeon begins the robotic right radical nephrectomy by medializing the colon to expose the renal hilum and kidney. The parietal peritoneum is incised along the line of Toldt, extending from the iliac vessels to the liver. The liver is retracted cephalad using a grasper clamped to the diaphragm or lateral abdominal wall. The left hand holds bipolar dissecting forceps, while the right hand uses monopolar curved scissors. The inferior vena cava (IVC) is exposed just below the liver.

The surgeon carefully dissects the duodenum from the anterior surface of the inferior vena cava (IVC), moving it out of the operative field. The posterior coronary hepatic ligament is incised to enhance access to the IVC. The renal hilum is then exposed using the top-down approach, which involves identifying the IVC under the liver and working down to the hilum.

The renal hilum is visible in the bottom right of the screen, with the renal vein entering the kidney and the pulsating renal artery located posterior to the vein. The surgeon further exposes the vein and explores the cranial portion of the hilum to check for additional arteries. A vessel branching off the renal artery is divided and clamped with a hemlock clip.
The surgeon isolates the right renal artery, marked by a green arrow, and applies multiple hemlock clips to control the vessel before dividing it.

An intraoperative ultrasound probe is inserted through a port to scan the renal vein and inferior vena cava (IVC) for thrombus presence. The ultrasound image is visible at the bottom of the screen, confirming that the thrombus in the renal vein does not extend into the IVC.

The surgeon visualizes the right renal vein, indicated by a green arrow, and divides it using a laparoscopic stapler. Both the renal artery and vein are now divided, with the edges of the renal vein trimmed to confirm a negative margin on frozen section. The stump of the renal artery is visible, pulsating.

The surgeon performs ligation of the right ureter using blunt dissection and electrocautery, with the ureter visualized and marked by a green arrow. Two hemlock clips are placed on the ureter, which is then divided. The dissection continues with the division of lateral sidewall attachments to mobilize the kidney, revealing the psoas muscle. The adrenal vein and remaining suprarenal attachments and arteries are identified and divided.

The surgeon performs paracaval and interaortocaval lymph node dissection. A lumbar vein is clamped with a hemlock clip and divided using bipolar forceps and scissors. Traction and blunt dissection reveal the intra-aortocaval nodes. Small bleeding vessels and lymphatics are cauterized, and hemlock clips are applied to prevent lymphatic leaks. Suction is used to maintain visibility of the operative field, and the IVC is gently manipulated to expose the paracaval nodes.
The surgeon uses an endocatch bag to remove the kidney and lymph nodes after releasing the paracaval and intra-aortocaval lymph node packets. The robotic system is undocked following the removal.
